preparation_method: dressed with lime juice and fish sauce

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Thai glass noodle salad with chicken is approximately 250 g, typically served as a single-plate meal or shared side dish in a Thai restaurant context.

nutrient_source.title: Yam Wun Sen (Thai Glass Noodle Salad) — nutrient profile estimated from USDA FoodData Central component ingredients: mung bean noodles (cooked), chicken breast (cooked), lime juice, fish sauce, vegetables, and chilli
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods and SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Thai glass noodle salad with chicken as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: cooked mung bean glass noodles (~55% by weight), cooked chicken breast (~25%), lime juice, fish sauce, shallots, chilli, coriander, and sugar (~20% combined). Values represent a weighted composite per 100 g of finished dish. FAO/INFOODS SEA tables consulted for regional ingredient cross-check.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — mung bean noodles / glass noodles (cooked) as analogous food; GI approximately 39–53 reported for mung bean vermicelli
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai glass noodle salad with chicken as a composite dish. GI of 45 is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate source: cooked mung bean glass noodles, for which the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) report GI values of approximately 39–53 (mean ~45). The addition of protein (chicken), acid (lime juice), and vegetables in the salad dressing is expected to further attenuate glycaemic response, supporting a GI estimate at the lower end of the mung bean noodle range. GL per serving = (45 × 41.25 g carbohydrate per 250 g serving) / 100 = 18.6.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a 250 g serving of Thai glass noodle salad with chicken provides approximately 1050 mg sodium, primarily from fish sauce, which represents a substantial portion of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced fish sauce or substituting with low-sodium soy sauce can meaningfully lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index from mung bean glass noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: Thai glass noodle salad with chicken has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 45, driven by the mung bean glass noodles which produce a slower postprandial glucose rise than wheat noodles or rice; the presence of protein from chicken and acid from lime juice further attenuates glycaemic response, making this a suitable occasional choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when portion size is controlled.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium from fish sauce and moderate cholesterol from chicken
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While the dish is relatively low in saturated fat (~0.4 g per 100 g) and contains beneficial micronutrients from vegetables and lime juice, the high sodium content from fish sauce (~420 mg per 100 g) poses a concern for cardiovascular disease management; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium preparation to keep sodium within heart-healthy dietary targets.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai glass noodle salad with chicken, known in Thai as ยำวุ้นเส้นไก่ (Yam Wun Sen Gai), is a popular Thai salad dish featuring cooked mung bean glass noodles tossed with minced or sliced chicken, dressed with a bold combination of lime juice, fish sauce, chilli, garlic, shallots, spring onions, and coriander. The dish is characterised by a bright, tangy, spicy, and slightly sweet flavour profile, with a light and slippery noodle texture contrasting with the tender chicken and crisp fresh herbs. It is served at room temperature or slightly warm, making it suitable as a main dish or shared side. Nutritionally, it is relatively moderate in calories (~112 kcal per 100 g), provides a meaningful protein contribution from chicken (~8.2 g per 100 g), and derives its carbohydrate content primarily from mung bean glass noodles (~16.5 g per 100 g), which have a low glycemic index of approximately 45. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. The primary nutritional concern is its sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g) from fish sauce, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, the low GI of the glass noodles and the protein and acid content of the dressing make this a favourable noodle dish choice compared to higher-GI alternatives. In Singapore, this dish is served at Thai restaurants and is available through food delivery platforms such as field survey.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Thai glass noodle salad with chicken as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component ingredient data using a weighted composition model. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database value for mung bean glass noodles as the dominant carbohydrate source. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level data sources are high quality and the dish composition is well-characterised.
notes: 1. The input food_name 'Thai ကြက်သားဆနိလှော်သုတ်' is a Burmese-language name for a Thai-style glass noodle salad with chicken. The canonical English name has been set to 'Thai Glass Noodle Salad with Chicken' and the Thai-script name ยำวุ้นเส้นไก่ (Yam Wun Sen Gai) has been assigned as the mandatory Thai local name per G6 multilingual rules. 2. The input cuisine tag 'Lor Mee' is inconsistent with this dish and has been disregarded; the dish is Thai cuisine. 3. The input food_group 'Grains and staples' has been retained as the dominant carbohydrate component is glass noodles. 4. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component data for this dish type. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component ingredients in the source database. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this dish composition. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed zero as this is a minimally processed dish with no hydrogenated fats in the ingredient profile. 6. GI estimated at 45 from mung bean glass noodle data in the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021; gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. GL per serving = (45 × 16.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 2.5 serving factor) / 100 = 18.6. 7. Halal status is marked false as fish sauce authenticity and chicken sourcing cannot be confirmed for the specific restaurant (Golden Cart Myanmar Restaurant). 8. The Burmese-script alias 'Thai ကြက်သားဆနိလှော်သုတ်' is retained in the romanized aliases section as it appeared in the original field survey listing and supports retrieval.
